data.title: Gasoline Futures Rise on Strong Demand
data.description:
US gasoline futures climbed above $2.15 per gallon, after data showed US gasoline inventories fell by nearly 6 million barrels to the lowest since early January, indicating robust consumer consumption.

Crude oil stocks increased by 1.5 million barrels, below market expectations, while reserves at the Cushing hub declined.

Meanwhile, markets weighed the potential impact of tariffs on Canadian oil, which could disrupt domestic supply and strain refining capacity in the Northern US. According to fuel distributor TACenergy, gasoline stations in New England faced higher costs on Canadian fuel shipments due to anticipated tariffs from US President Donald Trump.

In geopolitical developments, Ukraine agreed to a US-backed 30-day truce with Russia, while Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthis threatened to resume attacks on Israeli ships.

data.title: Mexican president says will wait before responding to US metals tariffs
data.description:
Mexico's President Claudia Sheinbaum on Wednesday said her government will not immediately retaliate to new 25% tariffs imposed by the U.S. on all steel and aluminum imports and instead will wait for a possible resolution in the coming weeks.

U.S. President Donald Trump's increased tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports took effect "with no exceptions or exemptions", escalating the global trade war.

"We will wait until April 2 and from then we will see whether our definition of reciprocal tariffs will be applied too," Sheinbaum said at her daily morning press conference.

Trump has argued that the tariffs are a necessary tool to restore the country's manufacturing capacity and create new jobs. Mexican officials have said the tariffs are unjustified, pointing to data that shows the U.S. runs a steel and aluminum trade surplus with Mexico.

The tariffs threaten an already fragile Mexican economy that is teetering towards a technical recession and faces the highest budget deficit since the 1980s. While Sheinbaum has proven to be an adept negotiator with Trump- achieving a pause on other tariffs- she may struggle to see the same results for steel and aluminum.

Trump imposed a 25% tariff on steel and a 10% tariff on aluminum imports in 2018 during his first term. The tariffs helped boost U.S. production and employment in those industries, but the impact was offset by negative downstream effects on sectors that relied on the metals. Trump lifted the tariffs on Mexico and Canada by mid-2019.

Mexico Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard has previously criticized the tariffs as unreasonable.

"It's unfair according to President Trump's own arguments. Because we, I repeat, have more (steel) imports than exports," Ebrard said at a press conference in February.

Mexico's steel chamber Cancero has warned the tariffs would hit three-quarters of Mexican steel exports worth $2.1 billion, and has called for retaliatory tariffs on U.S. steel.

data.title: Bank of Canada Cuts Again, But Easing May Slow From Here, Says ING
data.description:
The Bank of Canada has cut its policy rate 25bp to 2.75% on Wednesday, as expected, said ING.

This brings the cumulative amount of interest rate cuts to 225bps since June last year. This is despite surprisingly strong Q4 2.6% annualised growth after an upwardly revised 2.2% print for Q3 and stronger employment growth in the November-January period.

Instead, the BoC believes that Q1 2025 growth will "likely slow" with tariff concerns weighing on sentiment and activity, while February job growth has already stalled.

The BoC continues to acknowledge "more than usual uncertainty" due to United States trade tariffs and has a sense that this uncertainty is "restraining consumers' spending intentions and businesses' plans to hire and invest." After all, 76% of Canadian exports go to the U.S., equivalent to 20% of Canadian gross domestic product — so even a modest drop in exports could risk a recession, noted the bank.

As such, this latest move appears to be an insurance cut to try and protect against the downside risks for the economy, stated ING. However, with core inflation above target and inflation expectations creeping higher, the BoC warns that it will be "carefully assessing the timing and strength of both the downward pressures on inflation from a weaker economy and the upward pressures on inflation from higher costs."

It doesn't offer any new guidance on where policy is heading, but the bank assumes that the pace of cuts will slow after Wednesday's move and forecasts just one further rate cut coming in Q2.

The Canadian dollar was only marginally impacted by a consensus BoC rate cut and lack of forward guidance. If ING were to speculate on the content of the statement, the sentence that reads "monetary policy cannot offset the impacts of a trade war" may be viewed as a slightly hawkish signal compared with where rate expectations are.

Markets are still pricing in two rate cuts by year-end, with 10bps factored in for the April 16 meeting, pointed out the bank. This is more dovish than ING's baseline scenario, but a hawkish repricing in rate expectations looks unlikely until data offers some clarity on the U.S. tariff impact or the trade spat deescalates.

As a consequence, the bank sees any support from an eventual adjustment higher in the BoC terminal rate expectation only coming through in late spring, if not later. should continue to trade closely in line with U.S. tariff news.

Despite already trading around 2% above its short-term fair value, ING estimates some US dollar (USD) strengthening, leading to appreciation beyond 1.450.

data.title: Solstice Gold Seeking $0.85 Million in a Private Placement of Flow-Through Shares and Units and Share Units
data.description:
Solstice Gold Wednesday said it will look to raise $0.85 million in a private placement offering of flow-through shares, units and common-share units.

The company said it will raise $0.65 million from the sale of flow-through share units priced at $0.049 and made up of a tax-advantaged share and one half of a one-year warrant to buy a second share for $0.05. It will also sell priced at $0.04.

It will also offer $0.2 million worth of common-share units priced at $0.035 each and made up of a share and one half of a one-year warrant to buy a share for $0.05.

The majority of the proceeds will go to funding gold exploration at the company's Strathy Gold Project.

The company's shares were last seen up $0.005 to $0.04 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: GSK-Owned Viiv Reports Antiviral Suppression in HIV Therapy Study
data.description:
GSK's majority-owned Viiv Healthcare said Wednesday the phase 2b study found that N6LS antiviral kept viral levels suppressed in adults with HIV, when given in combination with cabotegravir long-acting.

In the study, N6LS was given every four months along with monthly cabotegravir long-acting to patients already stable on treatment. The antiviral was also well tolerated by the participants, the company said.

At the six-month primary endpoint, the study showed that 96% of participants receiving N6LS intravenously and 88% receiving it subcutaneously with rHuPH20 maintained HIV-1 RNA levels below 50 copies per milliliter, compared to 96% in the standard-of-care group, the company said.

ViiV Healthcare plans to progress a six-month intravenous formulation of the candidate in combination with cabotegravir long-acting for further evaluation in part two of the trial.

Pfizer and Shionogi are also shareholders in Viiv Healthcare.

data.title: Wall St mixed as tariff jitters overshadow inflation data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes: Dow down 0.48%, S&P 500 up 0.26%, Nasdaq up 0.90%
  • Intel jumps on report TSMC pitched JV to US chipmakers
  • PepsiCo drops on brokerage downgrade
  • February CPI rises 2.8%, lower than estimates

Wall Street's main indexes witnessed choppy trading on Wednesday amid concerns about the economic implications of an escalating global trade war, which eclipsed data showing U.S. inflation cooled in February.

A labor department report showed consumer prices increased less than expected in February, but the improvement is likely to be temporary , given the backdrop of aggressive import tariffs.

"This (CPI) is good news on the inflation front but obviously, with the imposition of tariffs, we still don't know where the real direction of inflation is at this time," said Peter Cardillo, chief market economist at Spartan Capital Securities.

Traders held on to bets that the Federal Reserve will proceed with a 25-basis-point interest-rate cut in June, according to data compiled by LSEG. The central bank is widely expected to maintain current borrowing rates at its meeting next week.

Investors did not rush back into riskier assets, with the largely unchanged Treasury yields reflecting the prevalent caution.

Trump's 25% protectionist tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports, which are likely to extend to copper , took effect and drew swift retaliation from Canada and the European Commission.

Ford and General Motors lost 1.5% and 1.3%, respectively, while an index tracking airlines (.SPLRCALI) lost 4.3%.

Steel and aluminum stocks bucked the downward trend. Cleveland Cliffs rose 8% and Alcoa added 2.5%.

At 11:39 a.m. ET the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 198.26 points, or 0.48%, to 41,235.22, the S&P 500 gained 14.74 points, or 0.26%, to 5,586.81, and the Nasdaq Composite gained 157.59 points, or 0.90%, to 17,594.35.

Consumer staples , among the best performers of the year so far, fell 1.8%, weighed by a 7% slide in Brown-Forman . Trade associations said retaliatory EU tariffs on the U.S. would be "devastating" for the liquor industry.

Technology stocks , among the top decliners so far in 2025, rebounded with a 1.4% rise.

Financial markets have been roiled by Trump's unpredictable tariff maneuvers, with analysts cautioning about potential capital outflows from Wall Street. Concerns are mounting that the new U.S. levies could stoke domestic inflation and possibly trigger a recession.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq recently entered correction territory, while the S&P 500 narrowly avoided confirming a 10% drop in the last session from its February high.

The uncertainty has prompted businesses to dial back investments and slash their forecasts.

Goldman Sachs lowered its 2025-end target for the benchmark index, while J.P.Morgan sees higher chances of a recession.

Intel jumped 3% after a report said TSMC had pitched Nvidia, Advanced Micro Devices and Broadcom about taking a stake in a joint venture to operate the U.S. chip company's factories.

The broader chips index rose 2.5%, aiding gains on the tech-laden Nasdaq.

PepsiCo fell 3% after brokerage Jefferies downgraded its rating on the stock.

Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.26-to-1 ratio on the NYSE, and by a 1.08-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted no new 52-week highs and 17 new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 16 new highs and 150 new lows.

data.title: TSX up 150 Points at Midday With Most Sectors Higher
data.description:
The Toronto Stock Exchange is up 150 points at midday, having lost more than 500 points over the previous two days. The Bank of Canada cut rates and the Canadian federal government detailed near $30 billion in retaliatory tariffs to help it fight a trade war with the United States after U.S. President Donald Trump just after midnight put 25% import levies on steel and aluminum against all countries, including Canada.

Most sectors are higher at midday, with energy (+1.7%) and info tech (+1.7%), leading, while telecoms, down 1.4%, is the biggest decliner.

The Bank of Canada as expected cut its policy rate by 25bps to 2.75%, noting that trade tensions with the U.S. will likely slow the pace of economic growth and increase inflationary pressures.

Veteran market watcher David Rosenberg noted while being mindful of the initial inflationary impact from the trade war, the Boc seems more focused on the downside macro risks. So, he said, the BoC will be cutting rates further from here and the Canadian dollar will be destined to reach or breach the January 2002 all-time low of C$1.6128.

According to Rosenberg, just to bring the Canadian economy out of its current excess supply (output gap) alone would mean a move weaker to C$1.55 in any event. All roads lead to a weaker loonie, which will be needed in any event to blunt the hit to the domestic resource and industrial sector, he said. So, Rosenberg added, plan for a 63 cent (U.S.) Canadian dollar, a 1.5% policy rate, and a 2.5% yield on the 10-year GoC bond as the fallout from the Trump-induced tariff war on America's so-called 'friend' north of the border.

And, Rosenberg said, focus your TSX exposure on the "bonds in drag" (Banks, REITs, Communication Services, and Utilities) and the beneficiaries of a weaker dollar (Travel/Tourism) and avoid the areas that are the most affected by the Trump trade action (Industrials, Materials, and consumer products).

In relation to the brewing tariffs war, Canada is levelling reciprocal dollar-for-dollar tariffs on U.S. steel and aluminum imports, in response to Trump's move. The federal government's latest countermeasures are worth near $30 billion and will come into effect at 12:01am tomorrow, Thursday. The 25% tariffs will hit steel products worth $12.6 billion and aluminum products worth $3 billion. In addition, Canada is hitting another $14.2 billion worth of imported U.S. goods with fresh tariffs. These measures are in addition to the $30 billion worth in tariffs that Canada has already imposed earlier this month, and all of that is part of the total $155 billion in tariffs planned by the Canadians.

On the U.S. front, Reuters noted U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said nothing would stop Trump's expanded 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum until U.S. domestic production is strengthened, and added Trump will add copper to his trade protections. Lutnick also told Fox Business Network that a meeting he plans to hold with Ontario Premier Doug Ford will seek to "lower the temperature" between the U.S. and Canada. However, Lutnick said he will wait for Mark Carney to be fully installed as prime minister before negotiating on trade at a national level. "So I think it's just to level-set things, make sure we know each other, and then we're going to negotiate with all of Canada," Lutnick said on the meeting with Ford.

data.title: Will Ethereum price bottom at $1.6K?
data.description:
Ethereum’s native token, Ether , dropped below $2,000 on March 10, and the altcoin has struggled to regain a position above the psychological level.

While Bitcoin and XRP exhibited minor recoveries over the past 24 hours, Ether prices failed to display bullish momentum in the charts.

The altcoin plummeted to a multi-year low of $1,752 on March 11. However, onchain data and technical analysis indicate that the price could drop an additional 15% in the coming weeks.

Ethereum dips below realized price after 2 years

The current price deviation below $2,000 carried onchain implications for the altcoin. According to Glassnode, a data analytics platform, ETH dropped below its realized price of $2,054 for the first time since February 2023.

ETH realized price calculates the average price of each ETH last moved, representing the average cost basis of the total circulating supply. The current drop below the realized price indicates widespread unrealized loss for all ETH holders.

The market value to realized value (MVRV) ratio also dropped to 0.93, indicating a 7% average loss for all ETH holders across the network. However, it is important to note that the realized price reflects the weighted average of all historical transactions. Hence, it encompasses the cost basis of every ETH holder, not a specific timeframe like 2023 to 2025.

Meanwhile, Ethereum’s total value locked (TVL) dropped to a six-month low of $45.6 billion on March 12, down 41% from its peak of $77 billion on Dec. 17, 2024.

Additionally, the total fees users paid to use Ethereum fell to $46.28 million—the lowest level since July 2020—further signaling weakening network engagement.

Related: Starknet to settle on Bitcoin and Ethereum to unify the chains

Ether price between $1.6K-$1.9K is “attractive”

In a recent X post, Glassnode explained how Ethereum’s cost-basis distribution could be useful in identifying potential support levels for ETH. Based on a weekly outlook, Ether’s recent drop below $1,880 led to an accumulation of 600,000-700,000 ETH around $1,900. The post states,

“This suggests $1.9K could establish itself as a support if $ETH consolidates at current levels. Above spot, $2.2K (465K $ETH) is the potential next resistance. The supply gap between $1.9K and $2.2K remains thin, making a short-term move towards resistance plausible.”

At the same time, anonymous analyst Ninja believes that the floor price for Ethereum remains between $1,600 and $1,900.

The trader added that the above range is an “attractive region for commercial money” and set a high swing target at $2,500.

Related: Bitcoin whales hint at $80K ‘market rebound’ as Binance inflows cool

This article does not contain investment advice or recommendations. Every investment and trading move involves risk, and readers should conduct their own research when making a decision.

data.title: Finland's Wulff to Start Change Negotiations Amid Organizational Restructuring
data.description:
Wulff Group said Wednesday it will start change negotiations with 60 employees, which could lead to the termination of up to nine roles.

The talks are part of the ongoing organizational restructuring for the Finnish workplace products and services company's Products for Work Environments segment.

The measures aim to improve Wulff's operational efficiency.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1148 ET - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1001 ET - Transcontinental is well-positioned to navigate a tariff situation, especially with its profits largely derived from Canada. In a CIBC report, Hamir Patel says about 10% of Transcontinental's revenue could be potentially affected by tariffs, the packaging company says a 25% tariff would hit Ebitda by about C$10 million-C$15 million--which is only 2%-3% of expected Ebitda for the 2025. Patel says that unlike some of its Canadian flexible packaging peers which predominantly have assets concentrated in Canada, Transcontinental has operations in both, "which may provide it with an advantageous position if there are tariffs going in both directions." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0733 ET - Advanced artificial-intelligence models can be developed without spending billions of dollars as evidenced by DeepSeek, Kaan Terzioglu, chief executive of digital operator Veon, says in an interview. Veon developed an open-source large language model in Kazakhstan for less than $2.5 million in about nine months, Terzioglu says. Veon developed the model in a consortium that included research institutes coordinated by the Ministry of Digital, Innovations, and Aerospace Industry of Kazakhstan. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0648 ET - Ukrainian telecommunications company Kyivstar decided to list in the U.S. as it is the market with the highest liquidity in the world, the chief executive officer of parent company Veon said. Kyivstar's listing choice reflects its understanding of the regulatory landscape and investors' expectations, CEO Kaan Terzioglu said last week during an interview at the Mobile World Congress, in Barcelona. "We believe that the liquidity, the confidence and the interest in America's stock market is far better than in other stock markets around the world," Terzioglu said. Veon Group delisted its shares from the Euronext Amsterdam exchange in favour of the Nasdaq in the fourth quarter. Kyivstar will make history as the first Ukrainian company listed in the U.S. stock exchange, Chief Executive Officer Oleksandr Komarov added.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0642 ET - Veon has committed to invest $1 billion in Ukraine over five years, Chief Executive Officer Kaan Terzioglu says in an interview. Terzioglu sees an opportunity to rebuild the infrastructure of the country, noting that the telecommunications group has been investing every day in the country. "In frontier markets, we have not invested in 5G, but Ukraine is a market where we will invest in the latest technologies in the rebuilding phase," he adds. Frontier markets are those whose economies are considered less mature than emerging markets. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0639 ET - Ukraine should be the top priority of bond market investors, Veon Chief Executive Officer Kaan Terzioglu says in an interview. The head of the telecom and digital services group expects a peace process that is going to take place in the next 12 to 18 months between Ukraine and Russia, which will pave the way for investors to participate in the next growth phase in Ukraine. Veon said in January that it plans to list its Ukrainian digital operator Kyivstar on the U.S. exchange Nasdaq. "Our company has been successful, profitable and is in a position to prove to investors that Ukraine will be a good investment," the CEO says. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0448 ET - Portugal's PSI 20 stock index falls after the government collapsed following a confidence vote. The PSI 20 is down 0.4% in a session in which most European blue-chip indexes are up. Retailer Jeronimo Martins, telecommunications company NOS and postal company CTT are the biggest decliners in the index, down 1.1%, 0.8% and 0.4%, respectively. Elsewhere in Europe, France's CAC 40 rises 0.9%, Germany's DAX climbs 1% and the broader Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.6%. Portuguese Prime Minister Luis Montenegro lost a confidence vote on Tuesday, having been in office for less than a year, potentially setting the country on track for a snap election. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0431 ET - The delayed rollout of an advanced Siri integrated into Apple Intelligence will likely moderate iPhone upgrades next year, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. Additionally, Apple could face some degree of higher input costs due to incremental tariff costs on China, the analysts says. "Without a 'killer AI app' in market ahead of the iPhone 17 launch, we don't see AI features contributing to accelerating upgrade rates as meaningfully as we did previously," they add. Therefore, the U.S. bank sees 2026 revenue and earnings per share 5%-6% lower, with $436 billion of revenue and $8 of EPS, 1%-2% below consensus. Shares closed 2.9% lower at $220.84. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0340 ET - Delivery Hero's $250 million deal termination fee from Uber Technologies will likely be taken positively by investors, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. The German food-delivery company confirmed that Uber would pay a $250 million fee after it decided not to appeal the Taiwan competition regulator's decision in December to block the sale of Delivery Hero's Taiwan business Foodpanda to Uber. The termination fee represents around 26% of the deal size, and around 3% of Delivery Hero's market capitalization, they say. Delivery Hero shares closed at 26.20 euros on Tuesday.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0313 ET - AI-driven personal financial advisors are set to replace traditional banking apps, Lloyds Banking Group's CEO of consumer relationships says at the MoneyLIVE Summit in London. "When we look ahead and we map out our future transformation road maps, smartphones and apps may no longer be the future. With the increasing use of AI and voice activation, the future is much more likely to be a personal advisor in your pocket," Jayne Opperman says at the banking conference. Once upon a time this was only available to wealthy private bank type customers, she adds. The U.K. bank is modernizing its core infrastructure, shifting to cloud-based systems while keeping its branch network to meet customers' shifting preferences, Opperman says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0213 ET - LG Electronics could post above-consensus 1Q earnings on increased sales of premium home appliances, Daishin Securities analyst Kangho Park writes in a note. The South Korean consumer-electronics company may also benefit from growing new businesses such as its home-appliance subscription services as well as its heating, ventilation and air-conditioning segment for corporate clients, Park says. He expects LG's operating profit to come in at KRW1.410 trillion for the January-March quarter, beating Daishin's earlier estimate of KRW1.140 trillion and the market consensus estimate of KRW1.200 trillion. Daishin raises its target price on the stock by 4% to KRW130,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW82,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0113 ET - Venustech Group's security business recovery may take some time, Nomura analysts say, noting the company's 2024 results. Declines in IT security budgets from government and enterprise customers weighed on Venustech's revenue growth in 2024, the analysts note. Also, the network security products provider's gross profit margin was diluted by increasing revenue contribution from security integration and services businesses with low margins. Although the company is leveraging artificial-intelligence capabilities on its security products and services, this will likely take time to be reflected in its fundamentals. Nomura cuts the stock's rating to neutral from buy and the target price to CNY20.00 from CNY28.00. Shares are 1.3% higher at CNY19.67. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0451 ET - Roche's optimized multiple-sclerosis drug Ocrevus dosing, coupled with compelling evidence of reduced disability progression, significantly increases the likelihood of treatment success, often overlooked by consensus, Intron Health analysts Naresh Chouhan and Dominic Rose say in a note. Intron Health analysts estimate the Swiss pharma giant's Ocrevus HD data to likely read out in the first half of this year. Ocrevus HD is expected to take 50% of Ocrevus volume by the time of biosimilar launches in the U.S. in 2029 and will grow beyond 2029, the analysts say. The broker upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from hold. Shares rise 3.9% to 308.00 Swiss francs. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0418 ET - Roche's deal to co-develop a weight-loss drug with Zealand Pharma makes sense as the Swiss pharma giant must expand its obesity therapy options to effectively compete in this field, Vontobel analyst Stefan Schneider says in a note. Roche entered a collaboration and licensing agreement with the Danish biotech company for its amylin analog petrelintide. It has only recently entered the weight-loss space through its acquisition of Carmot Therapeutics in December 2023. Vontobel expects the earliest launch for Roche's obesity assets in 2028 and anticipates risk-adjusted peak sales of 1.9 billion Swiss francs. Shares rise 3.7% to 307.50 Swiss francs. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1933 ET - Licensing income from the Daybue treatment for Rett Syndrome alone supports Neuren Pharmaceuticals's share price, meaning investors effectively get exposure to its next drug candidate "for free," Bell Potter says. Neuren is upbeat about the potential of NNZ-2591 as a treatment of several neurodevelopmental disorders. It has cited positive results from Phase 2 clinical trials in Phelan-McDermid syndrome, Pitt Hopkins syndrome and Angelman syndrome. Still, Bell Potter says it will be two years until Neuren's next major clinical catalyst: the first Phase 3 trial results for NNZ-2591. "Neuren has unrivalled balance sheet strength to prosecute future NNZ-2591 development yet effectively zero value is currently attributed to this asset, likely due to the Phase 3 readout timing," analyst Thomas Wakim says. Bell Potter retains a "buy" call on Neuren. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1902 ET - Health insurer Tricare's temporary suspension of payments to Zynex could last as long as a year, Chief Financial Officer Anna Lucsok told investors during an earnings call Tuesday. Tricare makes up about a quarter of Zynex's revenue, and the pause caused it to lower revenue guidance for the year. Zynex said it is unclear what specifically triggered Tricare's pause. Zynex is still in Tricare's network and if Tricare starts paying again, it would be responsible for paying all claims that it has processed during its halt, Lucsok said. Zynex shares fall 19%, to $5.65, after market close. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0458 ET - Banca Popolare di Sondrio's new plan envisages a radical change in shareholder remuneration, Equita says in a research note. The Italian lender pledged a dividend payout of 85% for the next three years. This compares to the broker's 70% ratio estimate and the company's 63% ratio for 2024, analyst Andrea Lisi writes. The new targets are broadly in line with his estimates for 2025 but higher for 2027 due to higher revenue generation and lower risk costs, he notes. The analyst adds that Equita's targets are typically more conservative than company guidance. Shares in Sondrio--which last month rebuffed a takeover approach from larger peer BPER Banca--rise 2.1% to 11.05 euros and have gained 36% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0448 ET - An acquisition of U.K. insurer Esure by Belgium's Ageas would be a positive step if the price is right but Ageas isn't likely to follow through, ING says in a research note. Reuters reported that Ageas and German peer Allianz are among the bidders for Esure, which is owned by Bain Capital, with a price tag of at least 1 billion pounds. "Price could go higher with bidding but Ageas is unlikely to chase this, since it is a PWC geared business (Price Web Comparison), when Ageas CEO has shown more interest in broker or direct distribution channel," analyst Jason Kalamboussis writes. If the price is around the 1.3 billion-pound mark, Bain Capital will sell at a small profit on its 1.21 pound billion acquisition in 2018, the analyst says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0442 ET - Legal & General is making strides as it enters a new chapter, Interactive Investor says in a market comment after the British group's 2024 results. "The progress of the group is almost swan-like, with gradual movements masking some furious paddling underneath the water," head of markets Richard Hunter writes, pointing to the streamlining of the business announced last June, which led to non-core disposals that will boost shareholder returns. Core operating profit rose 6% with contributions from each of its businesses, driven mainly by its institutional retirement unit, which has a busy pipeline for pension risk transfer deals. Shares trade 0.7% lower at 243 pence, paring some earlier losses, while the FTSE 100 edges up 0.2%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0349 ET - Legal & General's annual results are broadly aligned with consensus expectations, Jefferies says in a note after the U.K. group's update. The investment-services provider's 500 million-pound buyback is above a consensus estimate of 450 million pound and its 21.36 a share dividend is in line. Earnings roughly matched consensus with only low-single-digit variances across the board, analyst Philip Kett writes. "This is unsurprising as the market only recently heard from management, so any material misunderstanding the market had would have been highlighted then," he notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0313 ET - AI-driven personal financial advisors are set to replace traditional banking apps, Lloyds Banking Group's CEO of consumer relationships says at the MoneyLIVE Summit in London. "When we look ahead and we map out our future transformation road maps, smartphones and apps may no longer be the future. With the increasing use of AI and voice activation, the future is much more likely to be a personal advisor in your pocket," Jayne Opperman says at the banking conference. Once upon a time this was only available to wealthy private bank type customers, she adds. The U.K. bank is modernizing its core infrastructure, shifting to cloud-based systems while keeping its branch network to meet customers' shifting preferences, Opperman says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1943 ET - Insurance Australia Group appears to be better positioned than rival Suncorp in relation to claims from the recent ex-tropical cyclone that hit the country's east coast, according toGoldman Sachs analysts. They tell clients that while more claims are expected to flow from New South Wales state, it's possible that the Australian government's reinsurance program could leave IAG with claims costs within its maximum event retention. IAG has flagged 2,500 early claims, compared with about 7,800 at Suncorp. The government program could provide substantial protection for insurers, they add. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1921 ET - Morgan Stanley thinks investors are underestimating the earnings tailwind for McMillan Shakespeare from novated leasing of motor vehicles. Analyst Chenny Wang says it became clearer in February that companies and the public are looking harder at novated leasing opportunities. McMillan Shakespeare's novated orders were up 19% at the end of 1H, despite the company's year ago performance benefiting from a contract with the South Australia government. "We think this growth in earnings power has greater duration than what valuation implies," MS says. It rates McMillan Shakespeare at "overweight" with a A$20.00/share price target. McMillan Shakespeare is down 0.3%, at A$15.08, in early trading today. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1824 ET - Stitch Fix's investments in improving the quality of its assortment is resonating with clients, especially with male users, CEO Matt Baer says on a call with analysts. The online personal-styling services' men's category returned to growth in the latest quarter. "While a smaller percentage of our portfolio today, we are encouraged by the growth inflection and remain confident men's can be a durable growth engine for us going forward," Baer says. The company continued to see strength through February and March, and expects its active clients to be down quarter-over-quarter, but improving from the prior quarter. Stitch Fix is increasingly confident it will return to revenue growth in fiscal 2026, CFO David Aufderhaar says. The latest quarter marked Stitch Fix's smallest sequential decline in active client count in three years, Baer says. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1429 ET - A mix of current challenges in the economy and long standing ones in the US housing market present a potentially unique opportunity for home builders. Newly-built homes have been getting smaller since the pandemic, but potential buyers continue to be boxed out of the market as high mortgage rates have amplified affordability challenges. Smaller homes offer a potential solution for both sides, with Oppenheimer's executive director of equity research Tyler Batory noting builders can charge a lower price and in theory still get the same margin as a larger build. "If the demand is there, then all things equal, in theory, building smaller homes allows you to offer a little bit of a lower price point, you can reach a different targeted customer," adds Batory. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0945 ET - Oil futures are higher with some support from a softer-than-expected U.S. inflation reading that's keeping the dollar weaker, while the market awaits U.S. oil inventory data from the EIA. U.S. crude stocks are expected to have risen by 1.2 million barrels, according to a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts, with declines seen in gasoline and distillate stocks. OPEC left its oil demand growth forecasts for this year and next at 1.45 million barrels a day and 1.43 million b/d, respectively. The producer group said that so far trade tariffs "are not anticipated to materially impact current underlying growth assumptions," although they increase uncertainty. WTI is up 1.6% at $67.29 and Brent is up 1.4% at $70.51.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0719 ET - Harbour Energy's cash-flow from operations in 2027 is likely to be 40% lower than in 2025 despite just a 5.5% decline in production, Berenberg analysts write. After the Wintershall Dea deal, Harbour's exposure to European gas prices is higher and the analysts expect prices to fall from $12 per million British thermal unit in 2025 to $7.5 mmbtu in 2027. This is due to an increase in supply and means that operating cash flow should to fall to $2 billion in 2027 from $3.5 billion in 2025, they add. This decline is much sharper than the 5.5% reduction in Harbour's production base that the analysts expect. Shares fall 2% to 181.85 pence and are down 32% over the past year.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0157 ET - Manila Electric's earnings outlook appears positive, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. This outlook is supported by factors including sustained volume growth for the Philippine company's distribution business and the projected substantial capacity build-up for its generation unit, the analyst says. The expected capacity expansion from the power distributor's recent liquefied-natural-gas investment has prompted the brokerage to raise its 2025-2026 core EPS forecasts for the company by 7%-12%. SB Equities raises the stock's target price to PHP562.80 from PHP526.00 with an unchanged add rating. Shares are 3.0% higher at PHP499.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1745 ET - Meridian Energy's narrowing premium to other New Zealand power generators and retailers prompts Forsyth Barr to upgrade its stock to outperform, from neutral. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green notes that Meridian has traditionally traded at a 20%-30% premium to listed rivals. However, Meridian's share price has fallen more than 7% since the end of February, with only a small part of that attributable to it trading ex-dividend. "Meridian is offering the best value it has in over a year and now is a rare opportunity to buy the largest electricity generator/retailer," Forsyth Barr says, estimating its premium to peers is less than 10%. "While short-term earnings are under pressure due to dry hydrological conditions, this is temporary." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1546 ET - The Canadian dollar strengthens by about a half-cent after Ontario Premier Doug Ford defuses an intense day of trade tension by agreeing to drop a surtax on U.S.-bound electricity form the province. Earlier in the day, President Trump threatened to double planned tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum, to 50%, in response to Ontario's power surcharge, affecting customers in New York, Michigan and Minnesota. Ford says he dropped the surcharge after securing a meeting Thursday in Washington with Commerce Secretary Lutnick. "I'm a business person. I want to sit down and negotiate this and stop the bleeding as we're going at each other," Ford says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1531 ET - Crude futures settle higher despite some intraday selling pressure as the trade spat between the U.S. and Canada heated up then appeared to cool down again as Ontario Premier Doug Ford agreed to suspend a 25% surcharge on electricity exports to the U.S. after President Trump said he was doubling the tariff on Canadian aluminum and steel. "Near-term risks remain tilted to the downside," Forex.com market analyst Fawad Razaqzada says in a note, noting tariff risks for demand, OPEC+ output plans, and the incipient process to end the Russia-Ukraine war. But unless a major global downturn emerges, "the downside is likely to be limited for oil from hereand WTI could potentially bottom around the $60 area should it get there," he adds. WTI settles up 0.3% at $66.25 and Brent rises 0.4% to $69.56 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

By Anthony Harrup The U.S. Energy Information Administration expects crude oil prices to recover from February's losses as U.S. sanctions against Iranian and Venezuelan crude limit output, delaying a buildup in global inventories until the third quarter of the year.

"Although crude oil prices fell in February and were near $70 a barrel in the first week of March, we expect key upward price pressures will push the Brent price back into the mid-$70 a barrel range in the coming months," the EIA said Tuesday in its Short-Term Energy Outlook.

The agency expects Brent to reach $75 by the third quarter, averaging $74 a barrel this year and $68 a barrel in 2026. It forecasts U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate crude at $71 a barrel this year and $65 a barrel in 2026.

New U.S. sanctions on Iranian crude oil issued in late February have the potential to remove significant volumes of oil from the market, while the revocation of licenses for Venezuelan oil production and export to the U.S. will reduce the South American country's production in March, "tightening near-term oil market balances significantly" compared with the previous forecast, the EIA said.

"Global oil markets will remain relatively tight through the middle of 2025 before gradually shifting to oil inventory builds later this year," the EIA said. Increasing inventories are expected to put downward pressure on crude oil prices in late-2025 and through 2026.

The agency expects OPEC and its allies to raise output during the next two years, although the flexibility in the OPEC+ plan to gradually return withheld production beginning in April "leaves some uncertainty about whether increases will materialize in line with the announcement," the EIA said.

The agency attributed the February drop in oil prices largely to economic concerns related to trade tariffs announced by the U.S. and other countries, which has added to uncertainty about oil demand growth, while a potential ceasefire in the Russia-Ukraine war could bring additional barrels to the market.

The EIA expects world oil production to rise by 1.4 million barrels a day this year and by 1.6 million barrels a day in 2026. Consumption is seen increasing by 1.3 million barrels a day this year and by 1.2 million barrels a day in 2026. Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0646 ET - Endeavour Mining's free-cash-flow generation is about to pick up and enable the miner to increase shareholder returns, Berenberg analysts write in a note. The analysts expect the West Africa-focused gold miner to increase gold production by 9% in 2025 to 1.2 million ounces. The increase in gold production and the strong gold price environment should enable share buybacks of $80 million for the year in addition to its minimum dividend commitment of $225 million, the analysts write. In 2024, Endeavour completed $37 million of buybacks and delivered total shareholder returns of $277 million. Shares trade up 0.8% at 1,633.00 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0543 ET - Hochschild Mining investors will want clarity from its leadership team over medium-term production and unit-cost guidance, Barclays analysts write in a note to clients. The London-listed miner has confirmed the 2025 production, cost and capital expenditure guidance that it issued alongside its fourth-quarter production report in January. When first issued, it caused shares to tumble as cost and capital expenditure guidance exceeded analysts' expectations, as well as Hochschild's own previous guidance. This guidance will therefore be the focus of the results call later on Wednesday, they analysts write. Shares trade up 15.5% at 221.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0528 ET - Hochschild Mining's reintroduced dividend of $1.94 a share came in well ahead of consensus estimates of $1.30, Peel Hunt analysts write. The London-listed miner said Wednesday that it added 2.8 million gold-equivalent ounces of mineable resources in 2024. This will secure the miner's future cash flow and dividends, the analysts write. Hochschild announced a new dividend policy of 20% to 30% of free cash flow, combined with a minimum $10 million payout. This approach balances investor returns and investing in growth assets, the analysts write. Shares trade up 15.5% at 221.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0432 ET - Hochschild Mining's 2024 results were largely in line with expectations, RBC Capital Markets analysts Marina Calero and Laura Chan write. The miner restored its dividend and the analysts expect a positive reaction to the results. Hochschild's new dividend policy of 20% to 30% of attributable free cashflow could translate into an average annual dividend of $42 million over the nextthree years, they write. The addition of 2.8 million gold-equivalent ounces of mineable resources to its portfolio was impressive, they add. Shares trade up 8.2% at 207.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1821 ET - Ramelius Resources's refreshed plan for its Mt Magnet gold mine in Western Australia leads bull Shaw & Partners to trim its price target by 3%. Ramelius signaled that early years output would be lower than its earlier plan and all-in sustaining costs would be higher. That's due to the replacement of higher-grade Eridanus underground ore previously scheduled to be dug up in FY 2026 with the larger Eridanus open pit cutback in FY 2027. "The decreased production in FY 2026 and higher AISC in FY 2027 has resulted in a reduction of our target price from A$2.98/share to A$2.89/share," says analyst Dorab Postmaster. Still, Shaw retains a buy call on Ramelius, which ended Tuesday at A$2.31. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1730 ET - Heightened geopolitical and policy instability has eroded Jefferies's confidence in Nickel Industries's earnings visibility and growth outlook. Indonesia has proposed changes to its royalty structure that would drive a 15% decrease in long-term earnings from Nickel Industries's current projects, the bank estimates. The policy shift hasn't been legislated yet. Still, consultations follow changes in onshore earnings retention for 12 months from March 1, and the lowering of nickel ore mining quotas to 200 million tons from 240 million tons in 2024, analyst Mitch Ryan says. "We reinstate a 20% risk discount to net present value to account for rising macro uncertainty, heightened geopolitical and policy instability," Jefferies says. Its price target falls 19% to A$0.65/share. Nickel Industries ended Tuesday at A$0.605. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1434 ET - Front-month gold climbed throughout the day, with the most-active contract settling up 0.8% to $2,912.90 a troy ounce. Amid blows being exchanged between the U.S. and Canada over tariffs, gold is still positioned to set a new record, says Mizuho's Robert Yawger in a note. "Gold has been consolidating in the midst of mayhem within striking distance of the all-time high," says Yawger. He adds that even as investors seem hesitant to keep pushing gold up, prices remain close to all-time levels of nearly $3,000 an ounce. "It is still only one big day away from ringing the bell," says Yawger. SPDR Gold shares are up 1.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1548 GMT - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1520 GMT - Deutsche Post's cost-cutting program is positive as it offers the company a pillar of EBIT growth in 2026/2027, UBS analysts write. The program seeks to make longer-term and structural adjustments to the cost base and represents an acknowledgment that prospects for volume growth, at least short term, remain lackluster. After full-year 2024 results, the bank lowers 2025 EBIT estimates by 1% to reflect the lackluster volume growth expectations in the express business in a context of increased price competition from peers. UBS lifts its price target to 42.50 euros from 37 euros and keeps its neutral rating. Shares fall 0.4% to 41.63 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1417 GMT - German auto manufacturer Volkswagen is well-positioned to handle challenges in the auto sector such as tariff threats, making it possible for its bonds to deliver excess returns, CreditSights analysts say in a note. Volkswagen's recent labor agreement could enable it to lower its costs and the company is expected to benefit significantly from Europe's regulatory changes on emission standards, CreditSights says. The automaker has good geographic diversification, a strong balance sheet, resilient free cash flow, and great equity value, they say. "We still believe that Volkswagen is the most compelling place for investors to generate excess returns in the investment-grade auto's space." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1408 GMT - Volkswagen results confirm an inflection point, Deutsche Bank analyst Tim Rokossa writes. Fourth-quarter earnings were 19% ahead of consensus and the 2025 guidance calls for mid-single-digit upgrades, he says. "There could be further upside from a potential Co2 target change in Europe, cost cutting and model momentum." The bank believes the stock is still very much under-owned and sees the story continuing to work. The bank lifts its target price to 125 euros from 115 euros and retains its buy rating. Shares fall 0.5% to 110.70 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1405 GMT - Kuehne + Nagel is well run but has limited growth, Jefferies analyst Michael Aspinall writes. On an organic growth basis, Kuehne + Nagel and rival DSV are equally matched, which makes capital allocation the differentiator, he says. "On this basis, an in-line valuation versus DSV appears stretched for lower growth." Kuehne + Nagel is more likely to seek acquisitions near term. However, this may be limited to bolt-ons, Jefferies says. The bank prefers exposure to air transport over sea in 2025. With the largest exposure to sea freight and few inorganic growth catalysts, the bank starts the company at hold with a 240 Swiss franc price target. Shares fall 1.7% to 207.30 Swiss francs. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1352 GMT - Deutsche Post's express business is a prized asset that has seen capacity grow ahead of demand, Jefferies analyst Michael Aspinall writes. This has led to cost inefficiencies and lower margins, but as demand improves the bank expects strong positive operating leverage and in the meantime management are addressing costs. "The valuation is more than underwritten by express but eCommerce is growing and supply chain can soak up excess capital at a circa 15% return on invested capital." The stock is the bank's top pick in transport. Jefferies starts Deutsche Post at buy with a 60 euros price target. Shares fall 0.5% to 41.58 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1337 GMT - Volkswagen's lack of free cash flow generation versus peers can justify some of the recent pessimism, but Citi says investors have overextrapolated recent challenges. The bank expects Volkswagen's free cash flow will improve as previous strategic errors have been amended, while European and German sentiment continues to improve. On the other hand, Chinese competition and electric vehicle challenges will continue. "Considering some investors thought last year that VW would find it difficult to stay afloat for next 5 years - the full-year 2024 results show scant evidence of this." VW has a plan. Costs are being cut, wage growth will slow, and German VW brand capacity has been cut. 2024 results were solid while 2025 guide is mixed. Shares fall 0.8%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1128 GMT - Porsche still faces competitive challenges, leading the company to expect another transition year in 2025, Morgan Stanley analysts Javier Martinez de Olcoz Cerdan and Shaqeal A Kirunda write. The company sees 2025 as a year to undertake extensive measures to improve profitability, through the expansion of the product pipeline and improving availability of customization. Porsche will also adjust the corporate organization. Porsche has managed to gradually reduce its vehicle sales exposure to China to 16%, from around 32% in 2022, the bank says. "Investors have long been concerned about Porsche in China, and volumes may soon approach a lower bound at which this is no longer relevant." Fourth-quarter results were in line with lowered expectations following February's profit warning. Shares fall 4.4%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1050 GMT - Porsche investors will probably view the company's latest report negatively, as a return to better profitability will likely take several years, UBS analysts write. "The higher-for-longer capex and the commentary about a soft start into 2025 will likely be more relevant than the fourth-quarter print." The company's fourth-quarter operating profit is in line with expectations, but the margin missed while revenue was a 6% beat. The medium-term operating margin guidance range has been cut to 15%-17% from 17%-19% and the company guides for higher capital expenditure on investments in combustion engine and hybrid cars and battery activities. This will be a drag to free cash flow and margins also next year, UBS says. Shares fall 4.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1050 GMT - Rheinmetall's high ambitions for European NATO members' defense spending could allow the German arms maker to significantly beat even the highest of investor expectations for sales and Ebita in the coming five years, J.P. Morgan analysts David Perry and Lucy Fitzgerald say. "Could it be that RHM will be the best placed defense company in Europe for at least the next five years?" Rheinmetall says it could capture up to 20%-25% of the defense equipment spending by NATO members in Europe. The analysts plan to upgrade their sales and Ebita forecasts for the 10 European defense companies they cover, including Rheinmetall. Shares are up 7.3% at 1,237.5 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1030 GMT - Rheinmetall is clearly positioning itself to secure a dominant share of a special European defense fund under discussion, which could be worth about 200 billion euros, Mwb-research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck says. The German arms maker's 2024 results were "strong", with an all-time-high order backlog that could have been even higher if key projects hadn't been delayed to this year's first quarter, he notes. Rheinmetall is in a ramp-up phase, with the bulk of growth expected over the next five years, Rieck says. Its capital expenditure plans amount to 9% of sales, and are a clear signal that the company is preparing for a significant boost in orders, he notes. Shares are up 7.4% at 1,237.5 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0954 GMT - Rheinmetall's ambition to grow its defense business to more than 26 billion euros in 2030, versus around 8 billion euros in 2024, should be well received by investors, Berenberg analysts say. Revenue from the German arms maker's Vehicle Systems and Weapons and Ammunition divisions are both expected to exceed 10 billion euros in 2030, they note. Sales growth has been in line with the guided annual growth rate at the start of this year, they add. Shares are up 7.5% at 1,240 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq Rebound After Inflation Cools More Than Forecast
data.description:
US equity indexes traded mixed amid gains in the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq Composite after February's consumer price inflation eased more than forecast.

The Nasdaq Composite rose 1.4% to 17,688.2, and the S&P 500 climbed 0.7% to 5,612.1 after midday on Wednesday. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.1% to 41,378.4.

Technology, communication services, and consumer discretionary were among the four sectors that traded higher intraday, reflecting the impact of cooler-than-expected inflation data. Six companies out of the seven Magnificent-7 stocks were among the top 20 mega-cap gainers, with Tesla and Nvidia leading the pack. Consumer staples and healthcare were the steepest decliners, a signal that sentiment in the market is risk-on.

Data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics showed that consumer price index growth slowed to 0.2% in February from 0.5% in January Wednesday. Analysts in a Bloomberg-compiled survey expected a 0.3% increase. Annually, inflation cooled to 2.8% from January's 3% pace versus Wall Street's 2.9% expectation.

Core inflation, which excludes the volatile food and energy components, also decelerated to 0.2% from 0.4%, below the 0.3% consensus. From a year ago, core inflation came in at 3.1%, slower than the Street's forecast for 3.2%.

Most US Treasury yields rose intraday, with the 10-year up 1.9 basis points to 4.31% and the two-year rate climbed 3.3 basis points to 3.97%.

The CBOE's volatility index VIX, known as the fear gauge, slumped 4.3% to 25.84, extending its retreat from the highest in about seven months.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures jumped 1.7% to $67.41 a barrel.

data.title: Panther Metals Eyes Dual Listing on Canadian Stock Exchange
data.description:
Panther Metals is exploring a dual listing on the Canadian bourse, thanks to its favorable tax regime for explorers and growing interest in the company from North American investors.

As part of the dual listing plans, the London-listed mineral exploration company said Wednesday it agreed to settle its 150,000 pound-sterling unsecured convertible loan notes by issuing 362,250 new shares at 0.50 pound apiece, along with 362,250 warrants priced at 0.75 pound.

data.title: APEGA Welcomes Introduction of the Professional Governance Act
data.description:
Edmonton, March 12, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — The Association of Professional Engineers and Geoscientists of Alberta (APEGA) welcomes Bill 40, the Professional Governance Act, introduced in the legislature by the Government of Alberta on March 11, 2025.

Bill 40 will bring 22 professional regulatory organizations (PROs)—including APEGA—under one act. It includes the same overarching direction for all PROs, such as governance, accountability, professional practice, and professional conduct, while profession-specific schedules and regulations are developed for each.

“The Professional Governance Act provides a significant opportunity to modernize the legislation that regulates Alberta’s engineers and geoscientists—something APEGA has been focused on for more than a decade,” says APEGA Registrar and CEO Jay Nagendran, P.Eng., FCAE, ICD.D, FEC, FGC (Hon.). “It will make effective changes to our regulatory tool box while ensuring our century-long mandate remains the same: acting in the best interests of Albertans and public safety.”

At proclamation (the date this new legislation will come into effect) each of the organizations involved will see their existing acts repealed and formally replaced by the Professional Governance Act. APEGA will continue to collaborate with the government in developing specific regulations for engineering and geoscience, which will include scope of practice and authentication.

“At APEGA, we license, educate, and regulate. This legislation will enable us to do so more effectively and efficiently, so that engineers and geoscientists can remain focused on protecting the public interest while driving innovation and Alberta’s economy forward,” says Nagendran.

APEGA registrants and permit holders are encouraged to ensure they have up-to-date contact information in their myAPEGA profile to receive updates and remain informed of upcoming changes, progress on implementation, and how this legislation may affect them.

-30-

Links:

  • Government of Alberta news release: Modernizing Alberta’s professional regulatory laws
  • Government of Alberta fact sheet:  Professional Governance Act
  • Bill 40: Professional Governance Act
  • Read APEGA and ASET’s joint submission to government in 2019

ABOUT APEGA

APEGA is the regulator of engineering and geoscience in Alberta, safeguarding the public by enforcing professional and ethical standards. Our licensed professionals and permit-holding companies sustain a century-long legacy of innovation and economic growth in our province, inspiring the next generation of engineers and geoscientists.

data.title: Russia Inflation Rate at 2-Year High
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Russia quickened for the fourth month to 10.1% in February 2025, from 9.9% in the prior month and in line with market forecasts.

It was the steepest inflation rate since February 2023, driven by prices of food prices (11.7% vs. 11.1% in January), services (12.7% vs. 13%), and, to a lesser extent, non-food products (6.1% vs 6.1%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices rose by 0.8%, marking the smallest increase in four months.
